notes: New fights and updates discovered from web research covering February-March 2026. Focused on moratoriums, bans, permit denials, protests, and zoning restrictions across the US.

new_entries.summary: Ypsilanti City Council held a public hearing on March 3 to consider both a 60-day emergency moratorium and a separate 365-day resolution on data center zoning permits. The 60-day measure comes from city administration as a preventive step, while the 365-day resolution was introduced by Ward 3 Council member Amber Fellows. This is a separate action from the earlier Ypsilanti Township petition.

Boone Town Council discussed a moratorium on data center construction, with Mayor Dalton George saying the town should be proactive and 'recognize the environmental damage that these data centers and similar uses like crypto-mining can do.' A public hearing is scheduled for March 23, 2026, to further discuss the moratorium. Modeled after Canton's 12-month moratorium.

Columbus City Council discussed a proposed $5.18 billion, 900-acre data center campus (Project Ruby) in northeast Muscogee County near Harris and Talbot County lines. About half the citizens at the meeting voiced concerns about environmental and community impacts. A Change.org petition opposes the project. Developer Habitat Real Estate Partners selected Layfield Road for the facility, which would generate 195 permanent jobs and up to $68.7M/year in property taxes by 2030. Next public meeting is March 6, 2026.

Weld County commissioners are developing a data center zoning ordinance (Code Ordinance 2026-01) that would limit data centers to industrial or agricultural zones and require site plan or special review. Triggered by Global AI's purchase of 438 acres near Windsor for a data center. The county extended the timeline for the ordinance's final reading to allow more public input. Facilities under 50MW total capacity would fall under these rules.

Township supervisors voted 3-2 to reject a 120,000 sq ft, three-story 'boutique' data center on 18 acres off Route 724 before the proposal was even formally submitted. Over 100 residents attended, mostly in opposition. Developer Envision Land Use subsequently abandoned the plan entirely. The proposed site was at 299 W. Schuylkill Road, adjacent to Route 100 near a PECO substation.

Planning commission meeting was overwhelmed when too many residents showed up to oppose an expanded data center proposal, forcing officials to reschedule to March 9 at a larger venue. Sentinel Data Centers and Green Fig Land LLC seek to expand a previously approved data center by 61% to over 1.6 million sq ft on roughly 100 acres at 13 S. Bacton Hill Rd. The project straddles East and West Whiteland townships.
new_entries.company: Sentinel Data Centers / Green Fig Land LLC

new_entries.summary: Residents are challenging the validity of Tobyhanna Township's data center overlay zoning before the Zoning Hearing Board in Monroe County. The hearing is scheduled for March 11, 2026 at 5pm. Part of a broader grassroots resistance movement in Pennsylvania against over 50 proposed data center projects across the state.

Despite growing opposition, Middlesex Township Planning Commission approved Phase 1-A of a proposed $15 billion, 700-acre, 18-building data center complex (Pennsylvania Digital 1/PAX-1) on Country Club Road near Carlisle. Led by Carlisle Development Partners LLC. The plan goes to the Board of Supervisors in March. Would create ~450 jobs and generate $65M in tax revenue. Community concerns center on massive water and power demands in a rural area.
new_entries.company: Carlisle Development Partners / Powerhouse Data Centers

updates.summary: Birmingham City Council unanimously approved a 180-day moratorium on applications for new or expanding hyperscale data centers on March 3, 2026. Facilities under 20 megawatts are exempt. Two existing projects are grandfathered: DC Blox expansion and Nebius AI Factory on Lakeshore Parkway. A planning commission workshop is tentatively scheduled for March 11, followed by a public hearing March 18. New regulations could be ready for council vote by April 28.

Athens-Clarke County Commission is voting March 3 on whether to extend the current emergency moratorium (set to expire March 6). Commissioners are discussing regulations including closed-loop cooling requirements, on-site clean energy for facilities over 5MW, and potentially an outright ban on data centers. Commissioner Tiffany Taylor supports an outright ban and banning NDAs for data center proposals. Environmental groups released formal recommendations for data center rules in February.

Lordstown rescinded their original data center ban and replaced it with a 180-day moratorium. Bristolville LLC is fighting the village in court, claiming their application was filed before the ban/moratorium was enacted. The case has reached the Ohio Supreme Court. Council approved $400 for a consultant (Brian Frantz, Richfield Village planning director) to research and present on data center impacts.

After Columbia County approved 2 of 3 data center projects (White Oak approved unanimously, Pumpkin Center approved 4-1, Byrd Farms denied), a citizen filed two separate judicial review petitions challenging the two approved rezoning decisions. Both petitions allege procedural defects, incomplete analysis, and conflicts with the county's comprehensive plan.

Sedgwick County is preparing to extend its 90-day moratorium (set to expire April 17) to June 11, 2026. Two town halls scheduled: March 12 listening session and March 31 planning department session on zoning protocols. County leadership will propose the extension on March 4, 2026.

Monterey Park City Council directed staff to explore options for a permanent data center ban citywide. The Council is expected to consider extending the 45-day moratorium at the March 4, 2026 meeting to allow additional time for staff and the City Attorney to prepare permanent prohibition options. The original moratorium was approved January 21 after a 6-hour meeting with 300+ attendees and 100+ speakers opposing a 250,000 sq ft data center by HMC in Saturn Park.

Mason City Council is now weighing whether to repeal Ordinance 266 (M-3 Technology Innovation zoning district for data centers) or put it to a citizen referendum. A petition gathered 700+ signatures. Council is waiting on a legal opinion from the city attorney before deciding. Mayor blamed 'outsiders' for pushback, fueling further controversy.

Linn County Board of Supervisors unanimously approved one of the most comprehensive local data center zoning ordinances in the country on Feb. 18, 2026. Creates a new data-center-specific zoning district with requirements including comprehensive water study, water-use agreement before construction, 1,000-foot setbacks from residential zones, noise and light pollution limits, road damage compensation, and community betterment fund contributions. County planning director says no ordinance in Iowa asks for more requirements.

San Marcos City Council voted 5-2 at 2:14 AM on Feb. 17 to deny zoning amendments for a $1.5B, 380MW data center by Highlander SM One LLC (Fort Worth) on 200 acres on Francis Harris Lane. Over 100 residents spoke against it during 8+ hours of testimony. The overflow room roared with cheers when the denial vote came. Part of a broader fight in Hays County where 5 data centers have been proposed amid historic drought conditions.

Governor Pritzker announced a two-year suspension of state data center tax credit program in his State of the State address on Feb. 19, 2026. Takes effect July 1. Key agencies will study impact on energy grid and consumers. The data center industry generated $3.51B in tax revenue in IL in 2022-2023. The Data Center Coalition opposes the move, saying it puts IL at a strategic disadvantage. Democratic-controlled General Assembly must sign off.

The zoning text amendment ZTA 26-01 is advancing through the process. Public hearing held Feb. 24. The amendment would make data centers a conditional use only in industrial zones, require 500-foot setbacks from residential property, mandate Tier 4 EPA-compliant backup generators, and protect wetlands/streams. The Planning Board advanced the proposal in February. A competing bill would create a task force instead. Dickerson data center developers told they 'have a right to go forward' by county executive.

Rep. Darrin Madison introduced the 'Pause to Protect Act' in February 2026, proposing a moratorium on data center construction statewide until substantial changes are made to Wisconsin law. A separate bill would ban NDAs for data centers. Bipartisan community opposition has emerged in Mount Pleasant, Mount Horeb, Beaver Dam, Port Washington, and Janesville. Industry groups including Wisconsin Data Center Coalition registered in opposition.
